title: Non-Uniqueness of Smooth Solutions of the Navier-Stokes Equations from Critical Data

thm:1.1.summary: Koch-Tataru small data theorem: if divergence-free initial data has small $BMO^{-1}$ norm, there exists a unique global-in-time regular solution.

thm:1.2.summary: There exists divergence-free $U^0$ in $BMO^{-1}$ such that the Cauchy problem admits two distinct global smooth solutions, both in $C^\infty((0,\infty)$ x $T^3) \cap L^\infty([0,\infty)$; $BMO^{-1})$.

rem:1.3.summary: The constructed solutions lie in the Koch-Tataru path space $X_{KT}$, the natural space for $BMO^{-1}$ data — they are not perturbative around zero.

rem:1.4.summary: The $BMO^{-1}$ norm is critical under the Navier-Stokes scaling symmetry. Theorem 1.2 is the first ill-posedness result at critical regularity strong enough for small-data well-posedness.

rem:1.5.summary: The solutions are not Leray-Hopf (data not in $L^2)$. Construction uses a simplified mechanism from Palasek's dyadic model; the full mechanism should yield Leray-Hopf solutions in improved critical spaces.

rem:1.6.summary: The initial data $U^0$ is smooth outside a measure-zero set $\Sigma$, formed by the intersection of nested support regions with $|\Omega_k| \le 2^{-k}$.

rem:1.7.summary: The construction is on $T^3$ for convenience; extension to $R^3$ is expected straightforward, while $T^2$ faces geometric difficulties from Mikado flows.

lem:2.1.summary: Bernstein inequalities: for smooth multipliers obeying symbol bounds, Littlewood-Paley projections satisfy $L^p$ to $L^q$ estimates with the expected frequency-dependent gain.

lem:2.2.summary: Heat propagator estimates: frequency-localized data decays exponentially at rate $N^2$, with additional polynomial gains for homogeneous multipliers.

def:3.1.summary: Mikado flow vector potentials $\Psi_{j,k}$ and their heat evolution, plus the nested support regions $\Omega_k$ and $tilde{\Omega}_k$.

lem:3.2.summary: Properties of Mikado potentials: smoothness with $N_k-dependent$ derivative bounds, pressureless Euler solutions, disjoint pipe supports, and volume constants $A_{j,k}$ ~ $\delta$.

lem:3.3.summary: The nested support regions have exponentially decaying volume: $|tilde{\Omega}_k| \le 2^{-k}|T^3|$, with a cube-localized refinement for BMO estimates.

def:3.4.summary: Key operators for the construction: the symmetric gradient D, cutoff $\chi_k$, mollifier $\phi_k$, and Nash operators $\Gamma_j$ decomposing symmetric matrices near the identity.

def:3.5.summary: Inductive construction of $\psi_k^0$: the k-th iterate is a mollified superposition of Mikado potentials with amplitude coefficients $a_{j,k}$ depending on D $\psi_{k-1}^0$ via Nash operators.

lem:3.6.summary: One-step estimates: $||\psi_k^0||_{L^\infty} \le C_1 N_k^{-1} ||D\psi_{k-1}^0||^{1/2}$ and ||D $\psi_k^0||$ is controlled by ||D $\psi_{k-1}^0||^{1/2}$ with a gradient correction.

prop:3.7.summary: Inductive estimates: ||D $\psi_k^0||_{L^\infty}$ decays doubly exponentially (like 2^{-k}th power) with matching upper and lower bounds, and $||nabla^m \psi_k^0||_{L^\infty}$ ~ $N_k^{-1+m}$.

prop:3.8.summary: The sum of curls $sum_k curl(\psi_k^0)$ converges in $L^1$ and belongs to BMO, establishing that the constructed initial data lives in the critical space $BMO^{-1}$.

def:3.9.summary: The initial data $U^0 = curl(sum_k curl(\psi_k^0))$, the divergence-free velocity field in $BMO^{-1}$.

def:3.10.summary: The two principal parts $v^{(1)}$ and $v^{(2)}$: each splits modes into heat-dominated $v_k (forward$ cascade) and inverse-cascade-dominated bar{v}_k (nonlinear energy transfer), with even/odd parity swap.

rem:3.11.summary: The curl-curl operator produces divergence-free fields of the form div(D $\psi_k)$, which is essential for the Nash decomposition in Lemma A.1.

rem:3.12.summary: Formal verification that both principal parts $v^{(1)}$ and $v^{(2)}$ attain the same initial data $U^0$, using the Nash lemma identity and the fact that $\chi_{k+1} = 1$ on supp(D $\psi_k^0)$.

prop:3.13.summary: The principal parts $v^{(i)}$ are divergence-free with sharp $t^{-(m+1)/2}$ decay estimates and logarithmic $L^2-L^\infty$ integrability bounds essential for the perturbation argument.

prop:4.1.summary: Residual estimates: the Navier-Stokes residual $F^{(i)}$ from the principal parts is smooth and has arbitrarily small weighted $C^\kappa$ norm for large enough frequency parameter A.

prop:4.2.summary: Semigroup bounds for the linearized Navier-Stokes operator around the principal part: the propagator $S^{(i)}(t,t')$ maps Y into $L^\infty$ with quantified time-singular bounds.

prop:4.3.summary: Fixed point argument: for small $\epsilon_1$ and large A, there exist perturbations $w^{(i)}$ in $B_X(0$, $\epsilon_1)$ such that $u^{(i)} = v^{(i)} + w^{(i)}$ solve Navier-Stokes, with $w^{(i)}(t) \to 0$ as t $\to 0$.

lem:A.1.summary: Nash lemma: any symmetric matrix near the identity can be decomposed as sum of rank-one tensors M $= sum_j \Gamma_j(M)^2 \theta_j$ x $\theta_j$ with smooth bounded coefficient functions.

def:A.2.summary: The inverse-divergence operator R mapping vector fields to symmetric tensor fields, with the identity div(R V) = V - mean(V).

lem:A.3.summary: The operator R from Definition A.2 satisfies div(R V) = V - fint V, providing the key identity for the convex integration stress decomposition.

lem:A.4.summary: Stationary phase estimates for R applied to oscillatory functions: $R(a(x) e^{i \lambda k.x})$ gains $\lambda^{-1}$ with Holder corrections.

lem:A.5.summary: Product estimate for functions at separated scales: $||f(.) g(\lambda.)||_{L^1} \le ||f||_{L^1} ||g||_{L^1} + O(\lambda^{-1})$.

prop:B.1.summary: Existence of smooth solutions to the perturbed Stokes system (linearized Navier-Stokes around a smooth background v), establishing the semigroup $S^{(i)}$ is well-defined.

lem:B.2.summary: Generalized Gronwall inequality with both integral and $L^p-integral$ forcing terms, giving exponential-in-integral bounds.

lem:B.3.summary: Singular Gronwall inequality with (t-s)^{-1/2} kernel, yielding exponential bounds that absorb the time-singularity via an $L^p$ bootstrap.

lem:C.1.summary: General multiplier boundedness on the torus: $L^p$ to $L^q$ bounds for Fourier multipliers in terms of symbol norms and inverse Fourier transform norms.

edges.label: Main theorem follows from existence of two perturbations with same data
Needs principal part estimates to verify regularity and data attainment
Two principal parts v^{(1)}, v^{(2)} defined here
Uses doubly-exponential decay and derivative bounds
Uses cube-localized volume bound for BMO estimate
Uses cutoff chi_k and Nash operators
Properties of the objects defined in Def 3.1
Heat evolution of the inductive iterates
Uses doubly-exponential estimates on D psi_k^0
Computes residual from principal part definitions
Uses Mikado potential properties for v_k decomposition
Stationary phase for oscillatory residual terms
Heat propagator estimates for Duhamel terms
Singular Gronwall inequality to close the bootstrap
Principal part bounds feed into linearization coefficients
Needs semigroup bounds for the fixed point
Principal part estimates control nonlinear terms
Heat semigroup estimates for energy method
Derived from the more general Gronwall inequality
Bernstein inequalities follow from general multiplier bounds
Identity for the operator defined in Def A.2
Nash lemma motivates the curl-curl structure
Uses div(R V) = V - mean identity
Verifies data equality for v^{(1)}, v^{(2)}
Volume bounds on the support regions from Def 3.1

main_results.description: The main non-uniqueness theorem and its context within critical well-posedness theory.

mikado_building_blocks.description: Mikado flow potentials and their geometric/analytic properties: disjoint pipe supports, pressureless Euler, exponential volume decay.

inductive_construction.description: The Nash-style inductive scheme building $\psi_k^0$ from Mikado potentials, with doubly-exponential convergence of the symmetric gradient.

principal_parts.description: Definition and estimates of the two distinct principal parts $v^{(1)}, v^{(2)}$ that share initial data but evolve differently via even/odd mode splitting.

perturbation_argument.description: Construction of perturbations $w^{(i)}$ via residual control, semigroup bounds on the linearization, and a Banach fixed point argument.

convex_integration_tools.description: Nash lemma for symmetric matrix decomposition, inverse-divergence operator, and oscillatory/product estimates from the convex integration toolkit.

linear_pde_tools.description: Bernstein inequalities, heat propagator bounds, perturbed Stokes existence, Gronwall inequalities, and general multiplier estimates.

tao2016finite.title: Finite time blowup for an averaged three-dimensional Navier-Stokes equation
tao2016finite.relevance: Core predecessor: mechanism for non-uniqueness via dyadic models originates from Tao's averaged NSE construction.

palasek2024non.title: Non-uniqueness in the Leray-Hopf class for a dyadic Navier-Stokes model
palasek2024non.relevance: Direct predecessor by second author: proposes the non-uniqueness mechanism that this paper implements for the actual Navier-Stokes equations.

koch2001well.title: Well-posedness for the Navier-Stokes equations
koch2001well.relevance: The celebrated small data global well-posedness result in BMO^{-1} whose sharpness is proved by Theorem 1.2.

albritton2022non.title: Non-uniqueness of Leray solutions of the forced Navier-Stokes equations
albritton2022non.relevance: Non-uniqueness via convex integration with external forcing; this paper achieves non-uniqueness without forcing.

buckmaster2019nonuniqueness.title: Nonuniqueness of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equation
buckmaster2019nonuniqueness.relevance: Non-uniqueness of weak (non-Leray-Hopf) solutions via convex integration; contrasts with the smooth solutions here.

jia2014local.title: Local-in-space estimates near initial time for weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations and forward self-similar solutions
jia2014local.relevance: The Jia-Sverak program for non-uniqueness of self-similar solutions, a different approach to the same problem.

jia2015incompressible.title: Are the incompressible 3d Navier-Stokes equations locally ill-posed in the natural energy space?
jia2015incompressible.relevance: Conditional non-uniqueness result for NSE via spectral analysis of self-similar profiles.

isett2018proof.title: A proof of Onsager's conjecture
isett2018proof.relevance: Resolution of Onsager's conjecture; Mikado flows used here originate from this convex integration program.

bourgain2008ill.title: Ill-posedness of the Navier-Stokes equations in a critical space in 3D
bourgain2008ill.relevance: Norm inflation in B^{-1}_{infty,infty}, a weaker critical space; this paper works in the stronger BMO^{-1}.

hou2024regularity.title: On regularity of solutions to the Navier-Stokes equation with initial data in BMO^{-1}
hou2024regularity.relevance: Recent regularity results for BMO^{-1} solutions, complementary to the non-uniqueness shown here.

cheskidov2022sharp.title: Sharp nonuniqueness for the Navier-Stokes equations
cheskidov2022sharp.relevance: Sharp non-uniqueness results via convex integration in subcritical spaces.

leray1934mouvement.title: Sur le mouvement d'un liquide visqueux emplissant l'espace
leray1934mouvement.relevance: The foundational 1934 paper establishing existence of weak solutions to the Navier-Stokes equations.

nash1954c.title: C^1 isometric imbeddings
nash1954c.relevance: The original Nash embedding theorem whose decomposition technique (Lemma A.1) is a key tool in this construction.
